comparisons.visual: Notes: Period live-action Victorian fantasy; humans and talking animals share one stylized live-action medium. ALICE: about seven, fair hair, knee-length blue d…
She considers, solemn.
A long, low hall lit by a row of lamps hanging from the roof. Doors all round. Alice tries every one: all locked. She walks sadly down the middle. A little thre…
She shoots upward, opening out like the largest telescope.
She ticks off children she knows.
She runs to the little door - shut again; the golden key lies on the glass table as before.
The Mouse bristles all over.
They sit in a large ring, Mouse in the middle. Alice watches anxiously, sure of a bad cold if she stays wet.
The Dodo receives the thimble solemnly, then presents it back to Alice, saying -
Alice guesses at once: the fan and the white kid gloves. She hunts about good-naturedly. Nothing. The great hall, the glass table, the little door - all vanishe…
The bottle has had its full effect. She grows no larger - still very uncomfortable, wedged in the little room.
Long silence. Whispers.
She gives one sharp kick.
Another dead silence outside. Alice notices with surprise: the pebbles on the floor are all turning into little cakes.
She looks all round at the flowers and blades of grass. Nothing that looks like the right thing. A large mushroom grows near her, about the same height as herse…
"In my youth," Father William replied to his son, "I feared it might injure the brain; But, now that I'm perfectly sure I have none, Why, I do it again and agai…
Alice's head frees itself. She is delighted - until she finds her shoulders gone; all she can see when she looks down is an immense length of neck, rising like …
Alice stands before the door, wondering what to do next, when suddenly a footman in livery comes running out of the wood - the face of a fish, powdered hair dra…
She says it aloud, half to herself. The Duchess only scowls. The cook takes no notice.
Alice nurses the baby as she walks.
This time it vanishes quite slowly, beginning with the end of the tail, and ending with the grin, which remains some time after the rest of it has gone.
(aloud) I believe I can guess that.
The Hatter shakes his head mournfully.
He moves on. The Dormouse follows him. The March Hare moves into the Dormouse's place. Alice rather unwillingly takes the place of the March Hare. The March Har…
Once more she finds herself in the long hall, close to the little glass table.
The Knave only bows and smiles.
The Knave does so, very carefully, with one foot.
Alice walks beside the White Rabbit, who peeps anxiously into her face.
The King and executioner argue whether a head can be beheaded without a body. The Cat's head begins to fade away. By the time they have settled anything, the he…
As they walk off together, Alice hears the King say in a low voice to the company generally -
He sighs deeply, drawing the back of one flapper across his eyes. He tries to speak; sobs choke his voice.
The Gryphon shakes him and punches him in the back. At last the Mock Turtle recovers.
Alice looks down at her shoes.
The Panther took pie-crust, and gravy, and meat, While the Owl had the dish as its share of the treat. When the pie was all finished, the Owl, as a boon, Was ki…
The KING OF HEARTS and QUEEN OF HEARTS sit on their throne. A great crowd of little birds and beasts and the whole pack of cards assembles about them. The KNAVE…
The jury makes a memorandum.
One of the GUINEA-PIGS cheers and is immediately suppressed by officers: they slip him head first into a large canvas bag, tie it up, and sit upon it. Alice wat…
A general confusion. The Queen has the Dormouse turned out of court. The whole jury has to write down all the dates on their slates, and then add them up.
The King turns pale, and shuts his note-book hastily.
He turns to the Knave. The Knave shakes his head sadly.
